content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with a fat main character

**Score:** 126
**Body:** Im rewatching my mad fat diary and just want some good movies that also have a fat main character. Im ok with any genre and it can be old or foreign film. Thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tommy Boy
  - Black Sheep
2. Uncle buck
3. Heavyweights
4. Hairspray (the original as well as the 2007 musical remake).
5. Tucker and Dale vs Evil
6. Dumplin'

If you're in the mood for a series, Survival of the Thickest
7. Angus.
8. The Whale.
9. Precious (2009)

## 2. Looking for movies with interesting female leads, with emotional depth

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I began watching True Detective recently (I'm on the 3rd episode) and I was really intrigued by Matthew McConaughey and Woody Harrelson's characters, McConaughey's especially. They're wildly different, and Rusty's troubled and tortured vibe keeps me guessing and anxious to see what comes next.

While I was watching, I realized how male-dominated the show was. I was really enjoying it, but I was thinking back to some of my favorite movies and who the characters were. Trying to think of movies with captivating and complex female leads where romance isn't a main plot point was harder than I expected. There were a few that came to mind, but they felt like drops in an ocean of male movies.

Movies like Se7en, A Real Pain, The Thing, and Moon have male characters that are interesting. Their plots can be gritty, or painful, or confusing, and I feel like women are underrepresented in roles like that. I wanna see movies where female characters aren't just somebody's wife, or girlfriend, or a supporting role. I'm also not looking for a "girlboss" movie - they have their place, but I just want a movie that treats women with the same emotional depth and diversity as men.

I think that's enough yapping lmao. I'm sure there are many movies like what I'm looking for, and I would love all the suggestions I can get! Please make my movie catalogue a little more diverse!

Edit: Thank you all for the recommendations, I got way more responses than I expected!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival
  - Also Contact!
2. Philomena 

Blue Jasmine 

Living Out Loud

Ladybird

Muriel’s Wedding 

Everything Everywhere All at Once

Tv shows:

Mare of Easttown

Wandavision 

Beef

Dead to Me
3. Mad Max Fury Road. I will die on this hill. It's 100% a love story between Max and Furiosa. She is absolutely the lead here.
4. Off the top of my head...

- Thelma & Louise

- Terms of endearment

- Girl interrupted

- 9 to 5

Lil bit of everything in those and incredible actors in all of them. Sarandon, Parton, Fonda, Tomlinson, McClaine, Winger
5. The menu

Anora(she has no agency but there is emotional depth)

enola holmes

Triangle of sadness

Anatomy of fall

Gone girl
6. Midsommar (2019)

Promising Young Woman (2020)

Ghost World (2001)

Vivre Sa Vie (1962)

One Sings, the Other Doesn't (1977)

Poor Things (2023)

Mulholland Drive (2001)

The Witch (2015)

Kiki's Delivery Service (1989)

Persona (1966)

Petit Maman (2021)

Never Rarely Sometimes Always (2020)

Nights of Cabiria (1957)

Fish Tank (2009)

Secrets & Lies (1996)

The Nightingale (2018)
7. Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri (2017)

Nomadland (2020)

Eden (2012)

The Thing (2011) - since you mentioned The Thing, just in case you didn’t know about this. Strong female lead

Precious (2009)

Triangle (2009)


Also keep on with true detective season 1. It’s some of the best television, genuinely feels like a blockbuster movie that was cut into 8 episodes
8. Go!
  - Run Lola Run

## 3. Movies about just drinking/ beverages in general?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** Dark adult comedies like: “Sideways” (2004) starring Paul Giamatti & Thomas Hayden Church & Sandra Oh, “Leaving Las Vegas” (1995) with Nicholas Cage, and 2008’s, “Bottle Shock” all come into my mind, but anything else similar/ related to of the sorts/ similar to the sorts at all?

Thanks as always, fellow movie and cinema fanatics! 👍

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Barfly (1987)
  - Mickey Rourke is great in this.
2. Another Round, Wake in Fright
3. The World's End

The Angels' Share
  - Might as well watch the whole Cornetto Trilogy. Make a day of it with friends.
4. Strange Brew
5. Beerfest
6. The Golden Glove is a grimey booze fuelled horror movie based on a true story.
7. Beerfest
8. Bob and Doug McKenzie’s Great Wite North movie, Strange Brew.

## 4. Unique Genres

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I've been watching a lot of popular well known movies recently, but I feel like I have stayed in the very popular Genres like Horror, Action and comedy. Give me some suggestions for any movie that you feel is a unique genre. Preferably not stuff that is talked about a lot like requiem for a dream, try to keep it less popular stuff but ones you still like. Thanks I'm advance!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *10 Cloverfield Lane (2016)*

*Begin Again (2013)*

*Dark Blue (2002)* -- Prime

*Man on a Ledge (2012)*

*The Matador (2005)*
  - Dark Blue is such a solid pick, that one flew under the radar for sure. The Matador with Pierce Brosnan is wild too - dude plays this burnt out hitman having a midlife crisis and it's way darker than you'd expect from him
2. * Save the Green Planet! 
* 3-Iron
* Wild Tales (Relatos Salvajes)
3. Art House genre is my favorite genre. Movies like:

- Eraserhead

- Synecdoche, New York

- I'm Thinking of Ending Things

- Beau is Afraid

- Poor Things
4. Wings of Desire (Der Himmel Uber Berlin)


directed by Wim Wenders of recent Perfect Days fame 
  - i loved Paris, Texas & Perfect days. gotta see Wings of desire. Both Texas and Perfect seem boring at first but i love them. slow but sincere
5. Inland Empire (2006) — a three-hour mind fuck full of elements from drama, to fantasy, to horror, to surrealism, to thriller, etc.

It’s Such A Beautiful Day (2012) — an animated film with black comedy and psychological drama wrapped in a surrealist fashion.

No Country For Old Men (2007) — a Western film with elements of crime, thriller, and some horror with its violence.

Parasite (2019) — first half’s a black comedy, second half’s a straight up thriller.
6. *It's Such a Beautiful Day* by Don Hertzfeldt

I haven't seen another movie like it. It might resonate with you, you might love it, you might hate it. It's one of the most touching and meaningful movies I've ever seen, so I highly recommend!
7. Greener grass!!
8. Here's 2 completely different movies: 
Ain't Them Bodies Saints is an indie movie I've never heard anyone else mention. It's a rather solemn movie. Also Timeline is a movie that was based on the book by the same author who wrote Jurassic Park but it's not really known. 

## 5. Movies that best represent Washington state/different regions of Washington state?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** \*So this year I'm asking every state in the US for movie recommendations from their state, but since the Washington subreddit didn't let me post there, I thought of asking here\*

They could be from any time period, any part of the state, or any subculture that is specific to/prominent in Washington.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Twilight (being serious!)
  - Agree. 


Just beautiful. 
2. 10 Things I Hate About You 
3. Captain Fantastic (2015)
4. Sleepless in Seattle because there’s a lot of rain.
5. Twilight
6. Harry and the Hendersons
7. Singles. It takes place in Seattle during the Grunge years! I like the movie and the soundtrack is absolutely wicked!
8. Say Anything takes place in the Seattle area.
9. "50/50" did a pretty good job of depicting the Seattle area even though I believe it was shot mostly in Vancouver, BC.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Is this the best scream in movie history?

**Score:** 3198
**Body:** Home Alone

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His scream during electrocution is pretty good. I like that it fluctuate 
  - I have seen this movie hundreds of times.  Literally hundreds, and no matter what, this scene never fails to elicit honest to god belly laughs out of me.  I know it’s coming and I am not able to hold it back.  I even start laughing in anticipation of the laughter.  I love it so much.
    - When he flips to a skeleton for three seconds *chef’s kiss
2. The kid with tongue piercing in Rat Race when he's being pulled up the control tower. I cry laughing every time.
  - This movie is one of the greatest late 90s/early 2000s comedies. I love it
3. Ruby Rhod in Fifth Element
4. Fun fact. The scream you hear was recorded separately from the scene here. They used a real tarantula, and did not want to disturb it.
  - And done in one take, because Daniel Stern agreed to only put the spider on his face once.
5. Sam Rockwell is Galaxy Quest is pretty good.
6. Shatner in Wrath of Khan

## 2. Great actors who hadn't done anything noteworthy for years, and one role suddenly brought them back into the spotlight

**Score:** 1248

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Brendan Fraser
  - Ke Huy Quan from that same year too. Overall it was a very pleasant awards season
2. Winona Ryder Stranger Things

John Travolta Pulp Fiction

Ke Huy Quan Everything Everywhere All at Once
  - "Is that Data from the Goonies?"
3. Pam Grier and Robert Forster with "Jackie Brown".

David Carradine with "Kill Bill"?

Rutger Hauer with Batman Begins?

Tom Berenger with Inception?

Matthew Modine with The Dark Knight Rises?

Tarantino is better at reviving careers than Nolan.
4. Neil Patrick Harris in Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle.
  - It’s a fucking sausage fest in here. Let’s get some poontang, *then* we’ll go to White Castle.
  - Crazy what those 5-7 minutes did for him.  A decade of struggling in mostly random stuff.  Possibly not far from fading out.  But then briefly plays a hilarious coke & sex fiend and that shortly leads to him landing most actor's dreams.  Stable, big paycheck on a huge TV show
5. Can’t believe nobody mentioned Mickey Rourke in The Wrestler. Dude came back swinging.
6. I don't think anyone had Demi Moore winning a Golden Globe, Critics' Choice, and Screen Actors Guild award in 2024 on their Hollywood Bingo card.

## 3. Guillermo Del Toro’s Frankenstein (2025) is an unrecognizable adaptation, missing every mark that made Mary Shelley’s novel stand the test of time.

**Score:** 326
**Body:** ***Warning: Spoilers ahead!***

I read Frankenstein (or the modern Prometheus) for the first time a few weeks ago. I was excited to see GDT’s recent adaptation—that many have purported to follow the book closely—because I thought the text could be very well-suited to the screen. Reading some stretches is a bit of a slog, but the action is great and themes are thought-provoking. There’s a reason we’re still fascinated by this story over 200 years later, right? 

Imagine my surprise when the film uses only limited aspects of the book and recombines  them to an unrecognizable, nonsensical, reductive retelling somehow considered high-brow.

Let’s get the following out of the way: I understand that film adaptations must make changes to the original story to fit the different medium. I also understand that filmmakers can and should make choices to show their unique POV. However, GDT’s extreme choices stray so far from the original source material that it calls into question whether it is misleading for the film to be called “Frankenstein”, especially for viewers that have not read Mary Shelley’s novel.

The real problem with GDT’s movie is not that he made changes from the original story. It’s that the changes fly in the face of the book’s themes and strip it of all its complexity that gives it an iconic place in horror/sci fi literature.

There are literally countless ways the movie diverges from the book, but I will highlight the 5 that I think are most egregious:

**1. Victor Frankenstein engages with the creature right away.** In the 1818 novel, Victor comes down from his manic fury of experimentation when he realizes what he has done. The sight of his achievement frightens him and he immediately flees. This is important because a) Victor frets endlessly about his creation but doesn’t face it FOR YEARS and b) The creature’s first formative experience is rejection. This reaction sets up the arc of the rest of the story.

**2. The creature is framed for murder.** In the book, the creature is so hurt and lost from experiencing repeated, profound rejection that he systematically kills Victor’s loved ones. He expresses remorse and grief for his actions, but he does not know any other way to express his anger and compel Victor to create him a companion. In the movie, the creature does not intentionally murder anyone and is unfairly blamed. While GDT’s seeming entire goal of the film was to make the audience empathize with the creature and question the line between monster/human, I would argue erasing the creature’s mistakes robs him of his complexity and thus humanity. 

**3. The creature experiences romantic love from a human.** In the movie, Elizabeth meets the creature soon after he is created and is horrified by the way Victor has been treating him. See point 1, but we’ll go with it. The issue is not necessarily that Elizabeth has this early contact with the creature or even that she expresses sympathy towards him. The issue is that Elizabeth fully confesses her love to the creature and, while dying in his arms, tells him that she longs to be with him. This change makes the great tragedy of Frankenstein no longer make sense. In the book, the creature is always rejected by humans, hence his absolute desperation for a companion like him.

**4. Victor apologizes for his wrongdoing and calls the creature “my son” on his death bed.** The other great tragedy of Frankenstein is that Victor never really “learns his lesson”. It is an unsettling and dissatisfying ending. Even when the ship’s Arctic mission seems hopeless and he is clearly dying, he tries to encourage them to persevere. In the movie, he instead has a come-to-Jesus moment with the creature and tries to repent for how he treated him. He even directly tells the captain that he is telling him his story as a warning for being overly ambitious. Not only does this disrespect the message of the original novel, it also doesn’t make sense given movie version of Victor is much more intentioned in building the creature and more directly abusive from his inception.

**5. The creature frees the ship at the end.** GDT’s movie gave the creature regenerative healing capabilities. The book ends with the creature, over the body of his creator, woefully expressing his plan to kill himself and jumping in the water. With the creature being immortal, the movie necessitated a different ending. The creature instead frees the ship of the Arctic expedition stuck in ice. Similar to point 4, this takes a tragic ending and makes it pleasantly redemptive.

One of the most interesting aspects of the novel is the way the story is told primarily through letters and recounts, calling into question the reliability of each narrator. The movie clearly leaned into this, but in a way that simplified the story instead of promoting more reflection. In the book, both Victor and the creature are clearly flawed. This is precisely why it is still interesting to ponder who we consider to be the “monster.” But GDT took this beautifully nuanced and tragic tale and made it into a heavy-handed good guy/bad guy story, forcing his viewpoint onto the audience without agency. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh boy. Definitely don't watch any other Frankenstein film. 
  - Yeah, the critique is valid, but this recent Frankenstein is the closest to the book yet.
    - I agree entirely. I reread the book every October and I actually love this film because of how much of the book is included. 


It's my second favorite film adaptation of the source material at this point, and Karloff only wins out because that film is what I built my love of horror on. 
    - In terms of beat by beat following the plot, the Branagh version is the closest.

It is also a bit of a turd not just for the changes it makes but also because it's just tonally a total mess and Branagh seems to misunderstand every plot beat and character and that is constantly at odds with where the plot needs to go, but it definitely stuck closer to the book textually than GDT's version.

There simply hasn't been a truly faithful version that actually gets the novel - and I still loved GDT's version.
    - What about the Hallmark miniseries? According to a plot point breakdown by Cinemassacre, it's the most accurate to the book.
2. Del Toro, in a recent interview, said that he considers characters like Frankenstein’s monster, Dracula, and Pinocchio to be modern mythologies, and he feels no compulsion to slavishly follow the source material.
  - And I think that's completely valid. Which also means that adaptations can stray so far from the source material as to invert a lot of their major identifying points and subvert the originals. I think part of retelling and criticizing a story that's been told so many times is to understand how a new entry is in conversation with everything that preceded it and with the present moment. It's 2025—we don't need a faithful adaptation at this point.
3. I would **heavily** encourage anyone that is going to watch the movie, to first listen to his interview on Fresh Air, to understand Guillermo’s directing perspective about Frankenstein. I went in with an understanding that it would be his own creative take on the novel. Not a faithful adaptation. It is still an excellent, if not the very best take on Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein. Elordi’s acting was superb, it should win him an Oscar for the amount of emotion portrayed in such a short amount of time. The entire movie was beautiful. 10/10 (for me).
4. If you can find it, I'd recommend the stage production with Benedict Cumberbatch and Jonny Lee Miller.

Brilliant acting and from what I can tell very similar themes to the novel.
  - This is the best adaptation I’ve seen of this story for sure. I liked GDTs adaptation but agree with the OP that many of the themes for Shelley’s original work are diluted or outright removed. If you can find a recording of the play it is both incredibly visually striking (the sets and stage effects are immersive and creative) and still very faithfully presents Shelley’s original story. Also Cumberbatch and Miller, during the run of the play, took turns each night playing the doctor and the monster, so the acting of each character seems very unique in that both men understood completely both characters. Agree with this recco!!!

## 4. Name that time you found your first movie love . [ The Mask , Cameron Diaz ]

**Score:** 292

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think celebrity crushes are dumb and pathetic, and Sigourney Weaver at the end of Alien.
2. Rachel Weisz in The Mummy.
3. Salma Hayek walking across the street in Desperado 🤯
  - Oh, and From Dusk Till Dawn!
4. Merisa tomei in my cousin vini.
5. Kelly LeBrock, Weird Science.
6. Shannon Elizabeth in American Pie
7. Jennifer Love Hewitt - I know what you did last summer
8. Jennifer Connelly in the Rocketeer….
9. Natalie Portman in Star Wars

## 5. Who is this actress?

**Score:** 204

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shannon Woodward
2. She was Sabrina on Raising Hope, Dina on Last of Us 2, Didi on The Riches, and Elsie on Westworld.
  - Sabriner*
  - I loved the Riches. Such an underrated show.
3. Now I’ve got the wacka doo song in my head again
4. Raising hope is such a fun little comedy, it’s a lot like my name is earl, I think it’s the same guy? But it’s a good comfort easy watching show.
  - Yeah, the same people made it.  A lot of the cast from Earl make appearances in Hope.
  - If you liked those, check out Sprung.  Same creator, a couple of the same people.  Namely Martha and Garrett.  Only one season and I haven't heard about a follow-up, but a solid one season comedy.
  - Its set in the same universe! At the beginning of raising hope theres a news bulletin of earl
5. Shannon Woodward
